description: Designed to be gripped in one location (the grip) and readily fired with one hand
Longer weapons designed to be gripped in two locations and typically fitted with a buttstock to be braced against the shoulder
Typically features two hand locations and a buttstock but requires a bipod or mechanical mount for effective sustained fire
The inside of the barrel contains spiral grooves designed to spin a single projectile for accuracy
The inside of the barrel is a smooth tube, primarily intended to fire multiple projectiles (shot)
Used for niche or hybrid designs that may employ either barrel type or a combination of both
Uses the chemical energy from a fired cartridge to extract, eject, and chamber the next round (Self-loading)
Relies entirely on the user's physical manipulation to cycle the action between shots
A self-loading action capable of firing multiple shots with a single trigger pull
A self-loading action capable of firing only one shot per trigger pull
A wheel-like part with multiple holes behind the barrel
A hinge or "top lever" that allows the barrel to pivot away from the frame for loading
A lever with a knob that moves back and forth
A pivoting metal loop behind the trigger
A sliding grip on the front furniture (the wood or plastic parts of the gun). A sliding handle on the front is a giveaway for pump-action.
Arms that do not use self-contained cartridges and must be loaded from the front of the barrel
